title: The Macro Minute: Markets are allegedly “priced to perfection;” what could go wrong?

Happy Monday out there, team42. It's
your skipper here, Darius Dell to
present our macro minute for Monday,
September 22nd, 2025. I hope everyone
had a great weekend. So, as always,
we'll start with the executive summary
from today's leadoff morning note. If
you would like to review the analysis
supporting these conclusions and to see
if you need to make any changes in your
portfolio to remain on the right side of
market risk or to get on the right side
of market risk to the extent you haven't
been following KISS or Dr. Mo, you need
to be a client of 42 macro. So, today's
key macro question is, markets are
allegedly priced to perfection. What
could go wrong? Our answer. At face
value, investors seem exuberant with
global equities at record highs and
credit spreads compressed to 1998
levels. The S&P 500 has added 15
trillion in market value since early
April, recorded 27 fresh all-time highs
in 2025, and surged 34% in 5 months, a
feat topped only four times since 1950.
Underneath the surface, however, a
growing collection of investors is
warning the rally may have already
priced in a near perfect backdrop amid a
deteriorating economy. While we can only
speculate as to whether these are the
same underperforming bears that
myopically anchored on tariffs while
failing to maximize upside capture this
spring and summer. One thing is for
sure, global capital markets remain
supported by a fantastic risk-taking
backdrop. According to our deeply
researched view, there are only six
macro cycles that cause the momentum and
dispersion within and within and across
asset markets to change. growth,
inflation, monetary policy, fiscal
policy, liquidity, and positioning over
a medium to long-term time horizon,
which is 3 to 12 plus months in our risk
management nomenclature. Five of them
are improving and/or likely to be in a
better place than they are today. Only
one, the positioning cycle is
unsupportive and likely to be in a worse
place 3 to 12 plus months from now than
today. The only good news, the good news
is the positioning cycle is not a
catalyst in and of itself. Rather, it
merely acts as an accelerant when
developments in one or more of the other
five key macro cycles cause the momentum
and dispersion within and across asset
markets to change. Currently, we do not
anticipate materially adverse
developments in the other five key macro
cycles until at least next fall's US
midterm elections. Will there be bumps
and bruises to price in via corrections
along the way? Yes. But as we've been
communicating for months, dips are
likely to continue to be relatively
shallow until a material threat to our
paradigm C theme emerges. Transitioning
to our 42 macro dashboard. As always,
I'll wrap up with a question from our
community. This one's titled Rate Cuts
and Inflation. Says, "Hi, Darius. Thank
you for 42 macro. It has been incredibly
helpful for me. It took me many years in
my field to feel like uh feel like
because an expert knew so I couldn't do
this alone.
Sorry, my apologies. It took me many
years in my field to feel like I had
because an expert so knew I couldn't do
this alone." And I looked to find a
macro service I felt I could trust and
found that with 42 macro. So apologies
for the the misreading here. Uh I follow
along with the macroscott reports and
around the horns and think I understand
the ideas and that we need additional
rate cuts and can see value of that. I
have some friends I discussed investing
with and when I broach this idea I hear
that it's going to cause more inflation.
Good. And I'll tell you why good in a
second. I guess I should just be focused
on making money and not trying to
explain or discuss things to my friends.
But out of curiosity I want to see if
I'm understanding this right. Additional
rate cuts, say taking the Fed's rate
down to 3% done slowly enough not to
cause problems in the bond market should
not be a source of problematic inflation
because factors such as slack in the
labor market, need uh capital flowing to
areas of the economy and people where
it's needed, need to reduce mortgage
housing costs, growth isn't necessarily
inflationary, including the analysis
you've mentioned that the Philips curve
ideas are not right. Is that pretty
close? Help me educate my friends. So uh
obviously that's somewhat of a tough
question here in terms of u in terms of
how it was written but uh let me try to
unpack uh what I think I'm I'm being
asked here. So I think the key takeaway
the key the gist of the question is my
friends who are uh who see Fed cutting
rates now are getting incrementally
concerned about inflation and and
probably having some bearish u you know
take on asset markets as a function of
that. In our view that's wrong and it's
wrong in our opinion for a couple of
reasons. Number one reason is what are
the you know the Fed doesn't necessarily
cause inflation. Now the Fed definitely
contributes to inflation duh monetary
policy is a contributing factor to
inflation but as we've outlined uh in
our secular inflation model uh every
month since January of 2022 because of
there's so many different things that
have changed in the economy u relative
to you know the pre-COVID trend. it's
highly likely that the equilibrium core
PC inflation rate in the economy is now
somewhere in the high twos or low 3s.
Um, you know, we don't have to be
specific more specific than that because
I don't believe it's possible to be more
specific than that, but the reality is
if we now have an equilibrium level of
core PC inflation that's in the high
twos, low 3s, you know, the Fed's
reaction function just needs to evolve
to to sort of set that. You know, if you
think about monetary policy being the
longest leading indicator of the
business cycle, the Fed has choices to
make. The Fed can help engineer these
outcomes. But the reality is if we have
a level of inflation that you know that
is inconsistent with their 2% their
arbitrary 2% inflation target that they
got from the Reserve Bank of New Zealand
act in 1989. Uh if all those dynamics
are potentially pushing up inflation,
the Fed has a choice to make. Do they
want to push back on those dynamics and
create a level of inflation that is
inconsistent with everything that's
happening in the economy from a
political or demographic or or
technology or or geopolitical
perspective. you know, all those all all
those different things that are
happening. Um, or do they want to just
accept that and then try to guide the
inflation in the economy to maintaining
this 3% equilibrium level and obviously
not allow it to go uh much higher than
that? We think that would be the
appropriate policy and the policy that
will create the most favorable outcomes
from the perspective of the economy and
asset markets. And then the second thing
going back to what I was saying earlier,
the second thing that that suggests that
the Fed cutting interest rates here may
not lead to inflation is that the Fed
could be cutting interest rates into a
broad capex cycle. Obviously, we got a
tremendous amount of tax incentives uh
and in the um the one big ugly deal in
terms of you know trying to perpetuate
sort of a private sector supply side
driven uh capex and investment cycle in
the economy. uh and obviously the
administration's um you know substantial
deregulation they continue to guide to
and suggest is a high probability
outcome. If all that deregulation leads
to a robust capex cycle then it's likely
that any sort of credit growth we see
the preponderance of credit growth that
we see as a function of the Fed easing
monetary policy is likely to support
investment not consumption. When credit
growth goes to to support consumption
that is very explicitly inflationary.
However, credit growth that gets um that
that that is used to to capitalize
investment is obviously disinflationary
particularly over a longerterm time
horizon. You know, this is the sort of
argument that the supply side economists
tend to make is that hey, if you you
know, you create um the conditions to uh
you know facilitate a robust capex cycle
in the economy which I would argue many
of those conditions are in place and or
getting into place in terms of the one
big ugly bill tax provisions and the
substantial deregulation that is kind of
happening behind the scenes. If those
things are true, then cutting interest
rates into that suggests that it may not
be as inflationary as as as a lot of
investors may fear. Um, you know, I
think a lot of investors, you know,
quite frankly, and I don't say this
poratively, but it is true, this stuff
is very complicated. There are a lot of
very complicated dynamics that are
moving in the context of our secular
inflation model. There are a lot of very
context complicated dynamics that are
moving in the context of this economy.
There are multiple vectors of policy,
significant changes in policy on the
trade policy front, regulations front,
fiscal policy front, and obviously now
on the monetary policy front that are
all sort of, you know, hitting each
other at different levels and different
um different points that are combining
to produce really really complicated
outcomes in the economy. So obviously, I
think it's pretty easy for someone who,
you know, the knows less about this
stuff to say Fed cuts interest rates,
inflation's going up, this is going to
be inflationary. But the reality is I
don't know if that's the that's the most
appropriate outcome. In fact, that's not
the outcome we're betting on. We're
betting on at least over a, you know,
medium to long-term time horizon and our
risk management that, you know, this
stuff is unlikely to create the kind of,
you know, robust inflation dynamics that
would cause significant problems in
asset markets in terms of the forward
pricing for monetary policy. You know,
the Fed is not going to overcook the
books here for a variety of reasons in
our opinion. Uh, because for one, the
composition of the board hasn't changed
materially. You know, we think it's
going to be a much slower process for,
you know, structural dream change at the
Fed is not going to come overnight. It's
going to come as a function of a
multitude of of different policy makers
both within and around the institution
convincing the people that are on the
legacy Fed board uh and and and the
regional Fed bank presidents that, you
know, their views on all this stuff is
just, you know, quite frankly outdated
uh and their models need to to to
change. One of the models that needs to
change most notably going back to the
question the Philips curve there's no I
I've been doing this for almost two
decades I've been obviously I'm on my
like 18th00 chart in Bloomberg that I've
made since we started 42 macro almost
five years ago 18800 charts think how
many charts I make per day you know if
that's the case I I I' have yet to find
any analysis that shows me that the
Philip curve is true which by the way
this thing has been the principal
guiding principle from US monetary
policy since 197 78. Yet there's no data
that suggests that like having good
outcomes in the economy is inflationary.
That's just just that's just not it's
just not there's no data. I mean that's
just a academic theory that needs to go.
A lot of things that need to go
including the Fed's arbitrary 2%
inflation target, which you know, maybe
they never officially get rid of it, but
they could very well um you know re set
their reaction function in a way that
implies that they no longer are adhering
to it as evidenced by last week's uh
rate cuts. So I rambled enough about
this. if I wasn't clear, uh we'll we'll
pick this back up tomorrow or sometime
later in the week. So, everyone have a
wonderful day out there. We'll catch you
back here tomorrow. Cheers.
